museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: April brings Capcom's Commando into the arcade conversation and the UK magazine shelf starts to sharpen.
museum.curatorNote: April has arcade context, with some exact timing remaining source-dependent.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Commando appears in arcade release summaries
items.body: Capcom's top-down run-and-gun action becomes a cabinet hit and later a home-computer conversion prize.
items.sourceNote: Some summaries cite April 1985; regional timing varies.

items.title: Zzap!64 is about to land
items.body: Newsfield's Commodore magazine is poised to give C64 owners a distinctive critical voice.
items.sourceNote: Zzap!64 first issue cover dated May 1985.

items.title: UK players read across formats
items.body: Even loyal machine owners study rivals through reviews and screenshots.
items.sourceNote: UK magazine context.

items.title: Arcade action becomes a conversion economy
items.body: Publishers know that a cabinet name can sell tapes.
items.sourceNote: Arcade-to-micro conversion context.

items.title: Computer RPGs grow in the background
items.body: The year's role-playing releases are coming, but not every month leaves a clean trail.
items.sourceNote: Ultima IV and Bard's Tale arrive later in 1985.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors where the evidence supports them.

items.title: Commando
items.body: Capcom's run-and-gun war game becomes a conversion target for home micros.
items.sourceNote: Month is cited in some arcade summaries; keep regional timing cautious.

items.title: Gradius
items.body: Konami's side-scrolling shooter gives the power-up bar a lasting place in arcade memory.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames and other summaries place the arcade release in May 1985.

items.title: Hang-On
items.body: Sega turns the cabinet itself into a motorbike-shaped attraction.
items.sourceNote: Release timing varies by region; 1985 is secure.

items.title: Super Mario Bros.
items.body: Nintendo's platform game gives the Famicom a language of momentum, secrets and worlds.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is widely cited as 13 September 1985.

items.title: Ultima IV: Quest of the Avatar
items.body: A role-playing game concerned with virtue as much as victory.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames cites September 1985.

items.title: The Bard's Tale
items.body: A party RPG whose taverns, streets and dungeons make the computer feel like a boxed campaign.
items.sourceNote: Year is secure; month is not asserted here.

items.title: Gauntlet
items.body: Four-player fantasy action turns cooperation, shouting and hunger for food into arcade theatre.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames cites October 1985.

items.title: Little Computer People
items.body: A tiny resident in a digital house makes the computer feel oddly domestic.
items.sourceNote: October appears in several release summaries.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or technology contexts that explain the month's place in gaming history.

items.title: Commodore Amiga
items.body: Launched in July as a multimedia machine that immediately changes what screenshots can promise.
items.sourceNote: Computer History Museum cites 23 July 1985.

items.title: Atari ST
items.body: The other affordable 16-bit dream, shown early in the year and entering a staggered market.
items.sourceNote: Retail timing varies by region.

items.title: NES / Famicom
items.body: In Japan, Mario defines the Famicom; in the US, Nintendo cautiously tests the NES.
items.sourceNote: NES test launch and SMB sources.

items.title: UK 8-bit micros
items.body: Spectrum, C64, CPC and BBC remain the machines most British players actually use.
items.sourceNote: UK magazine and retail context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers

items.curatorNote: The CPC scene receives its defining dedicated magazine. Reconstructed placeholder, not a verified scan.

items.body: The CPC scene receives its defining dedicated magazine.

items.curatorNote: Newsfield gives Amstrad owners a sharper games-first identity. Reconstructed placeholder, not a verified scan.

items.body: Newsfield gives Amstrad owners a sharper games-first identity.

items.curatorNote: The Spectrum magazine remains a fixture of British games culture. Reconstructed placeholder, not a verified scan.
items.title: Crash
items.body: The Spectrum magazine remains a fixture of British games culture.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Before online gaming was ordinary, paper, shops and local conversations carried the culture.

items.title: Print is still the main network
items.body: Magazines, mail-order adverts and reader letters carry more practical games knowledge than online services.

items.title: Prestel and specialist services remain niche
items.body: Some UK computer users can imagine online information, but ordinary gaming culture is still mostly offline.

items.title: The playground is fast
items.body: Cheats, loading tips and rumours move through school faster than magazines can print corrections.

items.title: Demo and cover-tape culture is still forming
items.body: The magazine shelf is becoming a way to experience games before buying them, even before later cover tapes dominate.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: The future was unevenly distributed
items.body: April 1985 could mean reading about 16-bit marvels while still loading a tape on a rubber-key Spectrum.

items.title: Machine identity mattered
items.body: C64, Spectrum, CPC and BBC owners all read the same year differently.

items.title: Arcades still had the big magic
items.body: Hang-On, Gauntlet and Space Harrier made public play feel physically larger than home gaming.

items.title: Nintendo was not yet everywhere in Britain
items.body: Mario and the NES were historically seismic, but many UK players met them first as distant names.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for April 1985.
Month-specific dates are used only where the reviewed sources support them; uncertain items are marked in sourceNote/certainty fields.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
